feed.subtitle: Research Fellow at the Science Policy Research Unit (SPRU), University of Sussex Business School.

entry.title: Coworking Spaces as Vehicles for Place-based Skill Development in Left-behind Regions

link.title: Coworking Spaces as Vehicles for Place-based Skill Development in Left-behind Regions

entry.content: We examine how coworking spaces can serve as vehicles for place-based skill development in economically left-behind regions. Drawing on original fieldwork and survey data, we explore the role that coworking spaces play in fostering skill accumulation, knowledge exchange, and professional networking beyond major urban centres. Our findings suggest that coworking spaces offer a distinctive form of place-based infrastructure that enables workers in peripheral regions to access learning opportunities, collaborative networks, and entrepreneurial resources that would otherwise be concentrated in larger cities. By bringing together diverse workers — including freelancers, remote employees, and small business owners — coworking spaces create informal learning environments where skills are developed through interaction and shared practice. This research contributes to ongoing debates about regional inequality, the geography of skills, and the potential of place-based policy interventions to support economic development in left-behind communities across the UK and beyond.
entry.summary: We examine how coworking spaces can support skill development in left-behind regions.

entry.title: What UK Job Postings Reveal About the Changing Demand for Creativity Skills in the Age of Generative AI

link.title: What UK Job Postings Reveal About the Changing Demand for Creativity Skills in the Age of Generative AI

entry.content: We explore how generative AI technologies are reshaping the labour market, with a particular focus on creativity-related skills. Drawing on a large dataset of UK job postings, we examine shifts in employer demand for creative competencies over time. Our analysis reveals nuanced changes in how creativity is valued and defined across different sectors and occupations. As AI tools become more capable of performing routine and even complex creative tasks, the nature of human creativity demanded by employers is evolving. Some traditional creative skills are being displaced, while new forms of creativity — particularly those involving human judgement, originality, and the ability to work alongside AI systems — are gaining in importance. This research contributes to our understanding of the future of work and the role of creative industries in an AI-driven economy.
entry.summary: We explore Generative AI and the shift in labour market skill requirements.
